quoted_text:
HORMUZ TRAFFIC DROPS AS CEASEFIRE COLLAPSES

Ship crossings through the Strait of Hormuz fell to 25 yesterday, below the recent daily average of 30–50.

Traffic slowed after President Trump declared the U.S.–Iran ceasefire over and both sides exchanged further strikes.

Maritime analysts say the recent recovery in shipping activity has collapsed, with the Strait again operating under full conflict conditions.

quoted_text:
Yo, Goldman just turned positive on Glencore and the message is pretty clear: copper is back in focus

Goldman upgraded Glencore to Buy, saying the company is positioned well for copper, zinc and metallurgical coal strength. The key part for me is copper: tightening global mine supply, possible U.S. import tariffs and stronger demand from electrification, grids and infrastructure

That is exactly why $NRED / $NREDF stays interesting on the earlier-stage side. NovaRed has Wilmac in British Columb

quoted_text:
IRGC NAVY ANNOUNCES GRADUAL REOPENING OF STRAIT OF HORMUZ MARITIME TRAFFIC

The IRGC Navy has successfully managed the security of the Strait of Hormuz, resulting in a gradual reopening of shipping lanes.Maritime traffic capacity has currently reached approximately 50 percent of pre-conflict levels.

Vessels may continue to increase transit capacity provided they adhere to security protocols and receive authorization from the IRGC Navy.

The IRGC Navy maintains that foreign entities have no role

quoted_text:
Quick copper thought - Bernstein is balancing macro pressure with a tight physical market.

Higher energy costs, stronger dollar and rate expectations can pressure commodities short term. But copper still has support from reduced mine production guidance and U.S. inventory stockpiling.

That is the part I care about for $NRED.

If copper supply stays tight into the back half of the decade, companies with serious exploration angles become more relevant. NovaRed brings Wilmac, BC copper-gold-plati

quoted_text:
Ayo metals crowd, Bernstein is basically saying copper has macro noise but physical tightness underneath

Yes, higher energy costs, Middle East tension, a stronger U.S. dollar and hawkish Fed expectations can pressure commodities short term. But copper is not only trading on vibes

Bernstein also points to reduced mine production guidance and U.S. stockpiling tightening the physical market

That is the part I care about

By 2030, they forecast copper around $10,700/t as structural deficits start
